title: 1x3 Sawhorse Desk
summary: A small desk built on two miniature 1x3 sawhorse legs with hand-notched bottom shelf joints — a genuinely fiddly notching technique scaled down to desk size rather than full sawhorse size.
description:
This desk shrinks the sawhorse leg concept down to 1x3 stock and desk height, and its one genuinely tricky step is notching the bottom shelf boards into the angled legs by hand. **Use a precut beveled board as a physical guide to trace the notch shape** rather than trying to calculate and mark it from measurements alone — then cut the angled side of the notch with a jigsaw set to the matching bevel, and the straight cross-grain cut with a jigsaw or hand saw, not worrying if it notches slightly into the bottom board since the support boards added later cover any overcut. **Practice this notch on a scrap board first** and test-fit it before cutting your actual legs — this is explicitly the step worth a dry run.

Attach the bottom supports to the legs first, marking the insides of each leg 1-3/4" up as the reference point for that joint. Once the notched bottom shelf is fitted and attached, mark each leg 16-3/4" from the bottom for the second shelf joint and attach it the same way.

The top attaches last, with the legs marked and inset 1/2" in from each side edge — a small positioning detail that keeps the desktop from overhanging its own leg base unevenly. **The corner-notch pieces (1x12 and 1x6) explicitly assume standard board widths** (11-1/4" and 5-1/2") — the plan flags this directly and recommends adjusting the notch-out dimensions if your actual boards measure differently.

materials.name: Pine project panel, 24 in x 48 in
Primer and paint, or stain and poly

cutList.note: Both ends beveled 10° off square, ends parallel, long point to short point.
Both ends beveled 10° off square, ends not parallel, long point to long point.
Corners notched to fit around legs; assumes 11-1/4" actual width.
Corners notched to fit around legs; assumes 5-1/2" actual width.
Both ends beveled 10° off square, ends not parallel, long point to long point.

cutList.material: Pine project panel, 24 in x 48 in

steps.title: Cut the parts
steps.body: Everything but the top comes off the four 8 ft 1x3s, and every angled cut is 10° — set the saw once per configuration and run all the matching pieces at that setting. Cut eight legs at 29", both ends beveled 10° off square with the ends parallel, long point to short point, so all eight are identical. Cut four bottom supports at 9-3/8" and four second-shelf supports at 4-1/2", all with both ends beveled 10° but with the ends NOT parallel, long point to long point, which makes each a shallow trapezoid. Cut two bottom shelf ends at 24" from the 1x3, two shelf boards at 24" from the 1x12 and two more at 24" from the 1x6 — leave those six square and whole for now, since they get notched later. Those eighteen 1x3 parts pack into exactly the four supplied boards allowing 1/8" per cut, so cut the eight 29" legs first and take the short parts from the offcuts.

steps.title: Attach the bottom supports
steps.body: Each sawhorse is four legs splayed at 10° with supports between them, and you are building two of them, so four leg pairs in all. Mark the inside face of every leg 1-3/4" up from the bottom end — that mark is where the bottom support lands. Fasten a 9-3/8" bottom support between each pair of legs at that mark with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws and glue, letting the support's 10° bevels sit flat against the splayed legs rather than forcing them. Build all four pairs the same way, then stand each on a flat floor and confirm it sits without rocking before you go any further; a pair that rocks now will never come right later.

steps.title: Notch and fit the bottom shelf
steps.body: Use a precut beveled board as a guide to mark the notches on the bottom shelf boards. Cut the angled side with a jigsaw set to the matching bevel and the straight cross-grain cut with a jigsaw or hand saw. Practice on scrap first, then test-fit before cutting the actual pieces.

steps.title: Attach the bottom shelf
steps.body: With the notches fitting, drop the shelf boards down over the legs and onto the bottom supports: two 11-1/4"-wide 1x12 boards and two 5-1/2"-wide 1x6 boards, with a 24" 1x3 shelf end capping each outside edge — six boards, 38-1/2" across in total, all 24" long. Push them tight so the edges touch, then fasten down into the supports with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws, or glue and 1-1/4" brad nails. Do not worry if a notch overcut slightly into a board; the supports cover it, which is exactly why the notching step told you not to chase perfection there.

steps.title: Lay out the leg positions on the top
steps.body: Flip the 24" x 48" pine project panel face down and mark the leg positions before anything is fastened. Set each sawhorse in 1/2" from the side edge of the panel so the top overhangs its own base evenly rather than sitting flush, and trace round the top of each leg assembly so you can find the position again. The top does not go on yet — the second shelf is far easier to fit while the base is still open on top, so this step is layout only.
materials: Pine project panel, 24 in x 48 in

steps.title: Attach the second shelf
steps.body: Mark the inside edge of every leg 16-3/4" up from the bottom and fasten a 4-1/2" second-shelf support between each pair of legs at that mark, exactly as you did the bottom supports — the shorter support is what the 10° splay costs you higher up the leg. Use 1-1/4" pocket hole screws and glue. Lay a level across both sawhorses and check that all four supports sit at the same height before the glue sets. Note that this plan lists the second-shelf supports but no boards for the second shelf itself, so you will need to size that shelf off the finished frame.

steps.title: Attach the top
steps.body: Set the base upside down on the underside of the top, lined up with the marks you traced earlier, and fasten through the top of each leg assembly with 1-1/4" pocket hole screws — drill the pocket holes into the tops of the legs rather than driving anything through the panel face, so nothing shows on the desktop. Turn the desk right side up on a flat floor and check it with a level in both directions. If it rocks, shim under the low foot and re-check rather than forcing a screw down, which will only twist the frame.
materials: Pine project panel, 24 in x 48 in

steps.title: Finish
steps.body: Sand every surface, and pay particular attention to the leg ends — the 10° bevels leave a sharp point at each foot that will catch on flooring and on ankles. Ease the shelf edges and the top's edges too. Then prime and paint, or stain and seal with poly, giving the desktop an extra coat since it takes all the wear.
materials: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

images.alt: Small desk built on miniature notched sawhorse legs
